sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
print("M squares before:")
for n in previous_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"  center≈({center_r:.1f}, {center_c:.1f})")

print("\nM squares after:")
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"  center≈({center_r:.1f}, {center_c:.1f})")

# Check selector
print("\nSelector:")
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] == 16:
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"  center≈({center_r:.1f}, {center_c:.1f})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

OK, so clicking an M square without clicking a color square first still moves it up-left. The M square at (33.5, 15.5) moved to (32.5, 14.5). So the color square click is not necessary; clicking the M square directly moves it up-left.

- (32.5, 14.5)
- (38.5, 34.5)
- (54.5, 44.5)
- (55.5, 15.5)
The blue blob is at (27, 33).

Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game. Maybe the M squares are supposed to move towards the blue blob, but the movement direction is determined by something else.
Actually, I just realized something. The M squares are 2x2, and they're moving up-left by 1 cell each time. The blue blob is a larger object. Maybe the goal is to move the M squares until they touch or overlap with the blue blob.
But the M squares are moving up-left, which is not always towards the blue blob. For example, the M square at (32.5, 14.5) is to the left of the blue blob at (27, 33), so moving up-left would move it further away from the blue blob.
Wait, let me reconsider. The blue blob is at (27, 33), which means its center is at row 27, col 33. The M square at (32.5, 14.5) has its center at row 32.5, col 14.5. To reach the blue blob, this M square needs to move up (decrease row) and right (increase col). But it's moving up and left (decrease row and decrease col).
So the M square is moving in the wrong direction to reach the blue blob. This suggests that the goal is not to move the M squares to the blue blob.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to move the M squares until they form a specific pattern, such as a line or a square.
Actually, let me look at the original S dot positions again:
- (37, 18), (37, 41), (40, 37), (41, 16), (54, 49), (55, 14), (56, 47), (57, 16)
And the M squares formed at:
- (35.5, 17.5), (39.5, 35.5), (55.5, 15.5), (55.5, 45.5)
The M squares are at the centers of pairs of S dots:
- (37, 18) and (41, 16) → center ≈ (39, 17), but M is at (35.5, 17.5) - doesn't match
- (37, 41) and (40, 37) → center ≈ (38.5, 39), but M is at (39.5, 35.5) - doesn't match
Hmm, the M squares don't seem to be at the centers of S dot pairs.
Actually, let me reconsider. The M squares are 2x2, so they cover 4 cells. The S dots are 1x1, so they cover 1 cell each. When I click an S dot, it either becomes part of an M square or disappears.
Let me think about which S dots became M squares:
- S at (37, 18) → became part of M at (35.5, 17.5) - the M covers cells (35, 17), (35, 18), (36, 17), (36, 18)
- S at (40, 37) → became part of M at (39.5, 35.5) - the M covers cells (39, 35), (39, 36), (40, 35), (40, 36)
- S at (55, 14) → became part of M at (55.5, 15.5) - the M covers cells (55, 15), (55, 16), (56, 15), (56, 16)
- S at (56, 47) → became part of M at (55.5, 45.5) - the M covers cells (55, 45), (55, 46), (56, 45), (56, 46)
Wait, the S dot at (37, 18) is at row 37, col 18. The M square at (35.5, 17.5) covers cells (35, 17), (35, 18), (36, 17), (36, 18). So the S dot at (37, 18) is NOT inside the M square. This is confusing.
Actually, I think the M squares are not formed from the S dots. The S dots are just markers that indicate where the M squares should be created. When I click an S dot, it creates an M square at a nearby position.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. Maybe the S dots are "seeds" that grow into M squares when clicked. The M squares then move up-left when clicked.
Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just focus on moving the M squares and see what happens.
The current M squares are at:
- (32.5, 14.5)
- (38.5, 34.5)
- (54.5, 44.5)
- (55.5, 15.5)
If I keep moving them up-left, they'll eventually converge towards the top-left corner. But the blue blob is at (27, 33).
Wait, maybe the goal is to move the M squares until they overlap with the blue blob. Let me check if any M square can reach the blue blob by moving up-left.
- (32.5, 14.5) → moving up-left: (31.5, 13.5), (30.5, 12.5), ... - will never reach (27, 33) since col is decreasing
- (38.5, 34.5) → moving up-left: (37.5, 33.5), (36.5, 32.5), ... - might reach (27, 33) if it moves up 11.5 and left 1.5
- (54.5, 44.5) → moving up-left: (53.5, 43.5), ... - might reach (27, 33) if it moves up 27.5 and left 11.5
- (55.5, 15.5) → moving up-left: (54.5, 14.5), ... - will never reach (27, 33) since col is decreasing
So only the M squares at (38.5, 34.5) and (54.5, 44.5) can potentially reach the blue blob by moving up-left. The others will move away from the blue blob.
This suggests that the goal is not to move all M squares to the blue blob. Maybe the goal is to move only some M squares to the blue blob.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the M squares are supposed to move in different directions, and I need to figure out how to control the direction.
Wait, I just realized something. The color squares at the top might determine the movement direction, not just "activate" the movement mode. Let me test this more carefully.
Actually, I tested all 4 colors (S, M, p, Y) and they all caused up-left movement. So the color doesn't determine the direction.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the movement direction is determined by the position of the M square relative to the blue blob. For example:
- If the M square is above and left of the blue blob, it moves down-right.
- If the M square is above and right of the blue blob, it moves down-left.
- If the M square is below and left of the blue blob, it moves up-right.
- If the M square is below and right of the blue blob, it moves up-left.
Let me check:

- (38.5, 34.5) is below and right of (27, 33). According to the rule, it should move up-left. It did move up-left. So this rule works for this M square.
- (54.5, 44.5) is below and right of (27, 33). According to the rule, it should move up-left. It did move up-left. So this rule works for this M square.
- (55.5, 15.5) is below and left of (27, 33). According to the rule, it should move up-right. But it moved up-left. So this rule is wrong.
So the rule doesn't work for all M squares. The movement is always up-left, regardless of the M square's position relative to the blue blob.
OK, I'm stuck. Let me try a different approach. Let me move all M squares up-left until they reach the blue blob's row (27) and see what happens.
Actually, wait. I just realized that I've been clicking the M squares individually, which takes 1 action per move. But maybe I can move multiple M squares at once by clicking a color square and then... no, that doesn't work.
Let me think about the efficiency budget. I've used 36 of about 42 target actions. I have about 6 actions left. If I need to move each M square multiple times, I'll run out of actions.
Actually, let me reconsider the game. Maybe the goal is not to move the M squares at all. Maybe the goal is to click the S dots in a specific order to create the right pattern of M squares.
But I've already clicked all 8 S dots, and 4 became M squares. I can't go back and undo that.
Actually, wait. Let me check if the M squares can be "undone" by clicking them again. Maybe clicking an M square removes it or changes it back to an S dot.
Let me test by clicking an M square and seeing if it disappears.
Actually, I already clicked M squares multiple times, and they just moved up-left. They didn't disappear.
OK, let me try a completely different approach. Let me look at the history of Level 1 to see if there's a pattern.
Actually, I don't have access to Level 1's history in this session. Let me just focus on Level 2.
Let me try moving all M squares up-left until they reach the top of the board or the level completes.
Actually, I realize I should check if the M squares disappear when they reach the blue blob. Let me move the M square at (38.5, 34.5) towards the blue blob and see if it disappears when it reaches (27, 33).
The M square at (38.5, 34.5) needs to move up 11.5 and left 1.5 to reach (27, 33). But it moves up-left by 1 each time, so after 11 moves, it'll be at (27.5, 23.5), which is not (27, 33). After 12 moves, it'll be at (26.5, 22.5). So it won't exactly reach (27, 33).
Wait, the M square is 2x2, so its center is at half-integer positions. The blue blob's center is at (27, 33), which is an integer position. So the M square can never exactly overlap with the blue blob's center.
Hmm, maybe the goal is for the M squares to touch the blue blob, not overlap with its center. Let me check the blue blob's boundary.
Actually, let me just continue moving the M squares and see what happens. I'll move them until they reach the blue blob's vicinity.
But I'm running low on actions. Let me try to batch multiple moves.
Actually, I realize I should check if there's a faster way to move the M squares. Maybe clicking a specific location moves all M squares at once.
Let me try clicking the blue blob again, but this time check if the M squares move.
Wait, I already clicked the blue blob, and it only changed the timer bar. The M squares didn't move.
OK, let me try clicking the selector (yellow square) and see if it does anything.

- If the M square is below and right of the blue blob, it moves up-left.

So the rule doesn't work for all M squares. The movement is always up-left, regardless of the M square's position relative to the blue blob.

The M square at (38.5, 34.5) needs to move up 11.5 and left 1.5 to reach (27, 33). But it moves up-left by 1 each time, so after 11 moves, it'll be at (27.5, 23.5), which is not (27, 33). After 12 moves, it'll be at (26.5, 22.5). So it won't exactly reach (27, 33).
Wait, the M square is 2x2, so its center is at half-integer positions. The blue blob's center is at (27, 33), which is an integer position. So the M square can never exactly overlap with the blue blob's center.
